Now, let's get to our next core competency. You need is a story brand copywriter. That is how to write an email nurturing campaign. So here's what we're going to cover. First of all, what is an email nurturing campaign? What are the seven deadly sins of writing email copy? Seven things every email needs and the art of mastering email copy. So let's start with what is an email nurturing campaign? It's a series of emails and the reason this is important is because this may come as a shock to you, but not everybody is going to buy immediately from your website. I know it's hard to accept, but it's true. You need to have more contact with some people over a period of time before they make a buying decision. And just check this out in your own experience. Aren't there things that you've purchased either online or in the quote, real world that you had to think about and make several visits before you finally parted with your money? Of course. So it works the same way on the internet. It's a series of emails and in these emails, what is the content of these emails that you're going to send out over time? Well, you want to hit the major touch points of your copy on your main page. Think about what's happened. People have come to your website. They've maybe downloaded your interest generating PDF. They've read your copy. They've received a few emails from you, but they probably don't remember all the major parts of your sales copy. So you want to bring those major pieces of copy back to them in the form of email. In every single email, your objective is to bring them back to the website. Now, you're not going to do this overtly. Like your email should not say, come back to my website and buy my stuff. You need to be a little more subtle and elegant than that. But you want to make a point from your copy that's relevant to their world, to their pain, to their problem, and then remind them that you have the solution waiting back on your website. So an example would be from my own early career as a freelance copywriter. I had a website that sold my services. My sales letter was about me as a copywriter. That's the product that I was selling. So in my email nurturing campaign, I had a series of emails. I think there were about 21, 21 to 25. And each of those emails talked about one of the major points I made in my sales copy, pulled it from the sales letter, and every one of them ended with some invitation to come back to the website and fill out a form of interest to get me to write copy for their product or their service. Maybe you have a client that you're writing for and they're a sporting goods store. And maybe they specialize in softball gear. So you could send them a series of nurturing emails that touch on the major points from the copy about all the advantages of the kinds of softball equipment that you sell, maybe even tips about how to be a better softball player or league manager or coach or whatever the case may be. But every email leads back to the website with an opportunity for them to order something from that website. A single link, very subtle at the end, but this is what a nurturing campaign is all about. You're nurturing a relationship over time. So the next question that I'm frequently asked is, well, how many emails do I send? Is there a magic number? Should I send seven, 14, 21, 28? The answer to all those questions is yes. Don't get intimidated and think you have to write 100 emails before you actually start sending out your nurturing campaign. Start with two or three. Start with one. And one email at a time, you can always add a new nurturing email onto the end of the sequence until you build up a long sequence. It's okay to have 28, 25, 30 emails in the sequence, because when do you stop telling people about your product or service? Either until they buy or until they unsubscribe from your email list. But why wouldn't you, if they're on your email distribution list, why wouldn't you continue to talk to them and build a relationship? Because I've had people on my email list literally for years who never bought a thing. And then one day, in one case, seven years later, ended up buying something from me. And I went back and looked at the history and realized he'd been receiving emails from me for seven years. So don't put a limit on how many emails you need in your sequence, but don't feel like you have to write a dozen before you start. Start with one single email. One of the keys to a successful email nurturing campaign is to notice what's working and notice what's not. So you need to be able to go back and look at which emails get response, which emails don't. And in particular, watch for an email in your sequence where people tend to unsubscribe most often. Maybe it's email number five. If people are unsubscribing in a disproportionately high number on email number five, that tells you something in email number five is turning people off. I need to change that email. So notice what's working and do more of that. And notice what's not working and do less of that. Pretty simple, but something most people fail to do. Now let's talk about how to avoid the most egregious mistakes you can make when writing email, the seven deadly sins, if you will, of writing email copy. These are the things you definitely do not want to do. Number one, sin number one. The email is written from the company's point of view. I don't really have to elaborate on this, do I? It's not about you, it's about them. So you need to write these emails in a way that is meaningful to them and not just touting how wonderful you are. Sin number two is it feels like it's from a stranger. It feels like an ad, in other words. You definitely do not want to convey that feeling in your email copy. Sin number three is it looks like an ad. How many emails in your email inbox come with a banner or a coupon or a logo or something that you immediately know this is like an ad from a magazine, stuffed in my email inbox. And what do you do with those emails? I know what I do, delete. You want to avoid having your emails deleted, don't make them look like an ad. Deadly sin number four is your email uses we speak. It's the language that says we would like to tell you about what we have to offer from our vast storehouse of great stuff that we offer. It sounds like it's coming from a corporation or a big organization and people don't really relate to big organizations, they relate to other people. So you want to make sure your email has that feeling of coming from a person, not from we. Deadly sin number five. Deadly sin number five is your email talks at the reader. You know the feeling that you have when someone's talking to you, but they're really not talking to you, they're talking at you, they're lecturing you. They're pitching you, they're selling you, they're pushing you. You don't want to talk at your reader. You want to talk to them. You want it to feel like a conversation with the reader. Deadly sin number six, your email provides no value. If you send an email that is just a blatant pitch for your product and there's no value at all in it for the reader, and we're going to cover how you render value in your emails for the reader, that's coming up in the next section. But you want to make sure you're not sending emails that are of no value to the reader. They're only valuable to you. Like buy my stuff, you should buy it because it's great, and I need to have a sale and make some money. If you're conveying that feeling, people are not going to read or respond to your email. They're probably going to unsubscribe from your emails altogether. And deadly sin number seven is the email is spam. Now, even if it's not spam, if it's not unsolicited commercial email, they actually signed up for your email list, they actually confirmed it. If it feels like spam to the reader, it is spam as far as they're concerned. And really all that matters is how they feel about your email. If they feel like it's spam, they're not going to read it, they're not going to have good feelings about you, and they're not going to buy from you. So don't make your email feel like spam.
